# Organic-First Campaign Plan — Cohort AI Engineering Course

## 1. Campaign Ideas (5+ concepts across archetypes)

**Brief confirms all 8 fields. Proceeding in Mode A — no Assumptions table required.**

---

### Concept 1 — "The Room, Not the Curriculum"
**Archetype:** Community-Build + Referral Flywheel
**Thesis:** Senior engineers are drowning in AI tutorials but starved of peers who are doing the real integration work in production. This campaign markets the cohort bond — the 40 engineers who will save each other hours per week — not the course content, which is already commoditised by free resources. It converts alumni into the primary sales channel for cohort 2 and 3.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (founder LinkedIn + alumni WhatsApp/Slack node + word-of-mouth referral mechanics)
**Authenticity hook:** The 9/15 pilot referral stat is the campaign. That number — shared openly on LinkedIn as a post-mortem of cohort 0 — proves the room is what people recommend, not the syllabus.
**Minimum viable version:** Publish one 600-word LinkedIn post titled "Why 9 of my 15 pilot students came from someone else in the group" and measure comment depth and saves in 48 hours. If it passes the traction gate, build the referral mechanics from there.

---

### Concept 2 — "Open Curriculum, Closed Room"
**Archetype:** Counter-Narrative + Founder-Story Arc
**Thesis:** Every major cohort platform charges €5k+ for the promise of "comprehensive curriculum." This campaign counter-positions by publishing the full curriculum for free on GitHub — anchored to the existing 12k-star OSS project — and then selling access to the guided cohort experience that the free curriculum cannot replace. The refusal to gate the content is the message.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (founder LinkedIn + GitHub README + newsletter) → Tier 2 (earned media on "the engineer who gave the curriculum away")
**Authenticity hook:** The GitHub project already exists. The open curriculum release is a genuine action, not a PR stunt. The star count confers credibility no competitor can manufacture.
**Minimum viable version:** Add a "Guided cohort enrollment" section to the README with a short waitlist link. Measure GitHub traffic-to-waitlist conversion in 14 days.

---

### Concept 3 — "Engineer-to-Engineer Podcast Tour"
**Archetype:** Earned Media + Coalition Play
**Thesis:** Senior engineers trust other senior engineers, not marketing funnels. The founder guesting on 5–8 engineering-adjacent podcasts — speaking from her practitioner experience, not selling — reaches the exact audience (5–15 years experience, skeptical of hype) in the channel they already trust, at zero media spend.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 2 (earned media — podcast guesting) → feeds Tier 1 (newsletter and community growth from listeners)
**Authenticity hook:** She is a practitioner. She can talk about specific implementation failures, specific tools that underperformed, specific patterns that work in production — content hosts cannot get from a content marketer.
**Minimum viable version:** Secure one confirmed podcast booking in week 1 and publish the episode show notes as a LinkedIn post. Track newsletter signups from the show-notes link over 30 days.

---

### Concept 4 — "The Hype Audit"
**Archetype:** Counter-Narrative + Earned Media Stunt
**Thesis:** Senior engineers are fatigued by AI tool vendor claims. A monthly, public "Hype Audit" series — where the founder evaluates real AI-engineering claims against production reality — builds credibility by doing the thing the audience already wants to do but lacks the structured time for. It naturalises the course as the place where this critical thinking is applied to their own work.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (LinkedIn + newsletter) → Tier 2 (press pickup and engineer community reshares)
**Authenticity hook:** She has the production experience to make specific calls: "this vendor claim holds under condition X, fails under condition Y." Generic AI-scepticism content exists everywhere; practitioner-specific verdicts with named conditions do not.
**Minimum viable version:** Publish one Hype Audit newsletter edition and one LinkedIn thread summarising it. Track newsletter opens and LinkedIn saves vs. her average post. If both exceed her 7-day average by >30%, the format has legs.

---

### Concept 5 — "The 90-Day Transformation Brief"
**Archetype:** Referral Flywheel + Launch Push (cohort-timed)
**Thesis:** Every cohort launch is a moment that needs compressed attention. Each of the three launches uses the same engine: a "transformation brief" from 2–3 graduating-cohort engineers (what they shipped, what changed at work, what they told their colleagues) published 2 weeks before seats open, seeding social proof and referral share precisely when purchase intent is highest.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (alumni voices → LinkedIn + newsletter) → Tier 3 (boost the 1–2 transformation-brief posts that pass the 48-hour traction gate)
**Authenticity hook:** The alumni stories are real and specific: a named role, a named company type, a named production outcome. Not "I learned so much" — "I shipped the context-window optimisation we'd been debating for 6 months, in week 4."
**Minimum viable version:** For cohort 1, gather 3 transformation testimonials from pilot alumni (they exist — pilot ran with 15 students). Publish as a LinkedIn carousel with UTM-tagged enrollment link. Measure link-to-application conversion rate.

---

### Concept 6 — "Skeptic's First Week" (bonus concept)
**Archetype:** Founder-Story Arc (Self/Us/Now)
**Thesis:** The audience is explicitly wary of hype. A recurring founder-voice series called "Skeptic's First Week" — documenting the most confusing, frustrating, or underwhelming AI tool she encountered in the past week and what she actually did about it — speaks directly to their self-image as engineers who do not blindly adopt. It positions the course as the place where the skepticism gets productive.
**Primary channel tier:** Tier 1 (LinkedIn + newsletter) — purely organic, no paid component required
**Authenticity hook:** It requires genuine experience to write. The specific frustration, the actual workaround, the honest verdict. No competitor who is selling AI tools can credibly run this series.
**Minimum viable version:** Publish 4 consecutive weekly editions. Track newsletter subscriber growth rate and LinkedIn follower growth compared to pre-series baseline.

---

## 2. Selected Concept

The brief does not specify a selection. Per the skill's Mode A instruction, the plan proceeds with the **combination most consistent with the brief's bottleneck** (scale past existing network) and the cohort-education sector rider:

**Primary selected concept: Concept 2 — "Open Curriculum, Closed Room" as the campaign positioning backbone**, combined with **Concept 5 — "The 90-Day Transformation Brief"** as the cohort-launch engine for each of the three seat-open moments.

Concept 3 (Podcast Tour) is wired in as a reach-expansion channel under Stage 4. Concept 6 (Skeptic's First Week) becomes the weekly content cadence.

The four concepts together form one coherent narrative arc: the founder gives the curriculum away (credibility signal), documents her own skeptical practice (ongoing trust-building), deploys alumni voices at each launch moment (conversion signal), and earns media presence on engineering shows (reach past existing network). They do not require parallel separate execution — they are stages in one campaign.

---

## 3. Spend Asymmetry Verdict

**User's monthly budget:** €20,000 ÷ 8 months = €2,500/month.
**Competitor range:** Major platforms (effectively infinite for marketing); cohort competitors spend €5,000–€15,000 per cohort launch on ads, i.e., roughly €2,000–€6,000/month sustained.

Against the major platforms, asymmetry is **categorical** — no ad budget closes that gap. Against the cohort competitors specifically, the ratio is roughly 1:2 to 1:6 — bordering **mild-to-severe**.

**Verdict:** Your asymmetry is **mild against cohort-course competitors and categorical against platform giants**, which means you do not compete for platform search traffic at all — you compete for the trust and attention of engineers who already have opinions about where they get credible learning, and you beat the cohort competitors not by matching their ad spend but by being demonstrably more credible and more specific than they are. Paid spend is available for cohort-launch amplification only, on proven organic winners.

---

## 3a. Message-Market Fit Gate

**Question 1 — Revenue/commitment signal:** Have ≥10 people in the target audience paid, signed up, or directly asked for this without prompting?
**Answer from brief:** Yes. 15 students in a paid pilot. This clears the bar. ✓

**Question 2 — Language signal:** Can you name a specific pain the audience articulates in their own words?
**Answer from brief:** Implicitly yes — the brief states the audience is "feeling pressure to adopt AI tooling but wary of hype." The pilot ran and produced testimonials, which contain the audience's own language. This is borderline: the brief describes the pain accurately but does not quote the audience's own words. Treating as a soft ✓ — the first-30-days list will include extracting exact language from pilot alumni as a week-1 action.

**Question 3 — Close signal:** If you asked 5 people in the target audience to commit today, would ≥3 say yes?
**Answer from brief:** Yes. 9/15 pilot referrals came from within the cohort's own network — this implies a warm audience that would convert on a direct ask. ✓

**MMF verdict: 3/3 — confirmed.** The campaign is a distribution problem. The bottleneck is reaching engineers who do not yet follow the founder, not persuading engineers who do. Proceed to Stage 4.

---

## 4. Channel Tier Stack

**Primary function:** Trust compounding. The audience (senior engineers, hype-wary) can find the course once they encounter the founder's work — the bottleneck is the first encounter and subsequent credibility accumulation, not demand capture for an unknown category.

**Sector rider applied:** Rider 1 — Cohort-Based Education. Biases toward founder presence + community node + referral flywheel. "Market the room, not the curriculum" is the operative frame.

**Rider-function alignment:** Both the function choice (trust compounding) and the sector rider (cohort-education Tier 1 heavy) converge. No conflict. The podcast tour (Tier 2 earned media) is the primary reach-expansion mechanism to move past the existing 14k/4k follower/subscriber base.

---

| Priority | Channel | Tier | Weekly effort | Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Founder LinkedIn (personal, not brand page) | 1 | 3h | Trust compounding + reach past existing network |
| 2 | Newsletter to 4k subs (38% open rate) | 1 | 2h | Trust compounding + direct conversion |
| 3 | Alumni WhatsApp/Slack referral node | 1 | 1h (founder) + ops setup | Referral flywheel — the room sells the room |
| 4 | GitHub README enrollment funnel | 1 | 0.5h/week ops | Demand capture from OSS warm audience |
| 5 | Podcast guesting (engineering shows) | 2 | 1.5h/week (research + outreach + prep) | Reach past existing network |
| 6 | Organic LinkedIn (alumni transformation content) | 2 | 1h (ops coordination) | Social proof at launch moments |
| 7 | Boosted LinkedIn posts at cohort-launch windows | 3 | 0.5h + €300–500/launch | Amplification of proven organic winners only |
| 8 | Long-tail Google/Bing (non-brand intent queries) | 3 | 0.5h/week (ops) | Demand capture — "AI engineering course for senior developers" |

**Excluded:** Broad cold Meta/LinkedIn ad campaigns (no evidence of proven organic winner first; cohort competitors saturate this channel; the audience's ad-scepticism is an asset to preserve). Udemy/Coursera-category keyword bidding (categorical asymmetry against platforms on this axis).

---

## 5. 70/30 Allocation

**Organic (Tier 1 + 2): 75% of effort and 50% of budget.**
**Paid (Tier 3): 25% of effort and 50% of budget** — but paid is concentrated into three 2-week cohort-launch windows, not spread continuously. Between launches, the split is closer to 90/10 in favour of organic.

**Budget allocation (€20,000 over 8 months):**
- Podcast production/editing support (optional): €1,500
- Cohort-launch boosted posts (3 × €1,000): €3,000
- Long-tail Google Ads for demand capture (running continuously, capped): €4,000 (€500/month)
- Ops person's hours (10h/week × 8 months × implied rate): not a campaign cost, assumed included in brief
- Remaining €11,500: reserve for cohort 2/3 scaling of what works in cohort 1

**Rationale:** The founder's existing channels already produce meaningful reach at zero incremental cost. The €20k does not need to replicate reach — it needs to extend it past the 14k/4k base and amplify the three conversion moments. Over-spending in month 1 on cold paid before cohort 1 alumni provide social proof is the failure mode this sector (Rider 1) warns against.

---

## 6. Competitor Saturation Map

### Category A — Major course platforms (Udemy/Coursera model)

**(a) What they saturate:**
Generic search intent for "learn AI," "AI for developers," "machine learning course." They flood Google Ads and SEO with high-volume, low-specificity content — "everything you need to know about AI in 2025," "complete AI course," "beginner to advanced." Production is polished, branded, optimised for completion-rate metrics and platform algorithms. CTAs are urgency-driven ("Enrol now — 87% off today only"). The pricing model (€20–50 per course) forces volume, which forces generic audience targeting.

**(b) The absence that becomes your signal:**
Their €20 price point signals "this is mass-produced and interchangeable." Your €1,200 cohort price and refusal to compete on search volume is the message: this is not information, it is a structured peer experience that cannot be replicated by purchasing a course. Your curriculum published openly on GitHub — the same move that undercuts their value proposition — is the counter-positioning act. If they give you content for free, you give the content away for free and sell the room.

**(c) One-sentence positioning line:**
"The curriculum is free. You are paying for 40 engineers who will still be in your Slack in five years."

---

### Category B — Cohort-course competitors (US-based, $2k+ price point)

**(a) What they saturate:**
Paid LinkedIn funnels — promoted posts, sponsored InMail, retargeting to "software engineer" audiences — with polished testimonial carousels and outcome promises ("land your next senior role," "build AI features in 4 weeks"). Some use affiliate partnerships with newsletters in the engineering space. Content is aspirational and credential-focused. Production quality is high; the message is "transformation." Webinar funnels are common — a free live session that closes to a paid cohort.

**(b) The absence that becomes your signal:**
They spend €5–15k per cohort on ads before any student has arrived. Your refusal to run that funnel — visible in the absence of promoted posts and affiliate links — is itself a trust signal to a hype-wary audience. The founder's personal LinkedIn presence, posting engineering opinions and practitioner verdicts rather than lead-gen content, reads as categorically different from the polished-webinar funnel approach. The counter-positioning: no affiliate links, no countdown timers, no "only 3 spots left."

**(c) One-sentence positioning line:**
"We do not advertise because the engineers who need this already follow someone who knows us."

---

**Combined positioning line to hold across the campaign:**
"We publish everything we know. We charge for the room."

Every LinkedIn post, newsletter edition, podcast appearance, and alumni testimonial should be checkable against this sentence. If a piece of content cannot be summarised within it, it is off-message.

---

## 7. Three Alternative Campaign Shapes

### Shape A — Community-First

**Description:** Begin with the 15 pilot alumni as the founding community. Build a private Slack or WhatsApp group for past and prospective engineers. The founder posts daily for 30 days (engineering observations, practitioner verdicts, Skeptic's First Week content). Alumni are invited to post their own work. Cohort 1 enrollment opens to the community first, 2 weeks before newsletter/LinkedIn announcement. The community becomes the referral engine for cohorts 2 and 3.

**Flagship content (Self/Us/Now):**
- *Self:* "I ran a pilot with 15 engineers and 9 of the next enrollments came from inside the group. I didn't plan that. Here's what I think happened."
- *Us:* "We're all sorting through the same signal-to-noise problem. Most of what's published about AI engineering is either too early-stage or vendor-funded. We needed a room where the people were honest about what actually worked."
- *Now:* "I'm opening this group to 50 people. If you're a senior engineer trying to sort real AI integration from the hype, come in. When cohort 1 opens, group members get first access."

**Tradeoff:** Slowest to scale past the existing network; highest conversion rate from people who do join; most durable referral asset after cohort 3. Risk: if the community does not reach 50 engaged members before cohort 1 opens, it is a small amplifier. Requires 30 consecutive days of founder presence (non-negotiable; the channel dies without it).

---

### Shape B — Earned-Media-First

**Description:** Prioritise podcast guesting and 1–2 high-reach engineering publications as the primary reach mechanism. The founder targets 8–12 engineering podcasts and newsletters in the European senior-engineer audience. Each appearance is anchored to a specific practitioner story (a production failure, a tool verdict, a pattern that worked). Appearances drive newsletter signups and GitHub stars, which become the warm audience for cohort enrollment. The open-curriculum release on GitHub is the earned-media hook.

**Flagship content (Self/Us/Now):**
- *Self:* "I published our entire course curriculum on GitHub last week. Someone asked why I'd give it away. Here's the honest answer."
- *Us:* "Every senior engineer I know has built or evaluated an AI integration in the last 18 months. Most of us have made the same three mistakes. The curriculum documents them. The course fixes them together."
- *Now:* "The curriculum is at [github link]. Cohort 1 opens in March. If you want to work through it with 39 other engineers and direct feedback, the waitlist is here."

**Tradeoff:** Fastest to reach engineers who do not yet follow the founder; lowest direct control (relies on third-party booking and editorial decisions); each successful appearance has a long tail (podcast episodes accumulate listens over months). Risk: podcast bookings take 4–8 weeks to land and air; cohort 1 timeline is tight. Podcast tour is the best mechanism for cohorts 2 and 3, not cohort 1.

---

### Shape C — Search-Capture-First

**Description:** The existing GitHub project (12k stars) is an organic SEO asset. Redirect OSS traffic toward cohort enrollment via a structured README pathway and a dedicated landing page targeting long-tail queries: "AI engineering course senior developers," "practical AI integration for software engineers," "AI tooling for backend engineers." Combine with a small, permanent Google Ads budget (€500/month) targeting non-brand, high-intent queries where cohort-competitor paid presence is lighter.

**Flagship content (Self/Us/Now):**
- *Self:* "The most common question I get from engineers who find this repo is: 'how do I actually deploy this pattern in my company without it becoming a science project?' That question is why I built the course."
- *Us:* "If you've starred this repo, you've already identified the right problem. The gap between 'this looks right' and 'we shipped it' is what the cohort addresses."
- *Now:* "Cohort 1 starts [date]. GitHub sponsors get priority on the waitlist. [Link]"

**Tradeoff:** Most defensible over time (SEO compounds); does not require sustained founder attention at Tier 1 volume; leverages an existing asset (12k-star repo). Slowest to compound (SEO takes 3–6 months for new content to rank); GitHub-to-enrollment conversion requires careful funnel design; less differentiated as a brand narrative. Best as a background channel in parallel with Shape A or B, not as the primary shape.

---

**Recommendation for sequencing across 3 cohorts:**
- **Cohort 1 (first ~2.5 months):** Shape A (community-first) as primary, Shape C (search-capture) as background. Alumni from pilot give the community immediate credibility.
- **Cohort 2:** Shape B (earned-media) as primary — by this point, there are cohort 1 testimonials and the curriculum is live. Podcast appearances now have a strong hook.
- **Cohort 3:** All three shapes running in parallel. Referral flywheel from cohorts 1 and 2 is doing the heavy lifting.

---

## 8. First-30-Days Action List

**Total capacity:** Founder 8h/week + Ops 10h/week = 18h/week combined.
**Campaign phase:** Pre-cohort 1 launch. Goal: 60+ qualified waitlist applications to fill 40 seats.

**Draft effort totalled 22h/week. Cuts applied to fit 18h/week ceiling:**
Cut: long-form case study writing (move to month 2). Cut: Twitter/X presence (audience is LinkedIn-dominant; not worth splitting attention). Cut: Facebook Group monitoring (audience is not there at seniority level). These are the actions to re-add if capacity increases or if LinkedIn engagement data suggests the audience is elsewhere.

---

### Week 0 (Pre-launch: before public announcement)

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Launch private Slack/WhatsApp community; write rules; personally invite 20 pilot alumni and 10 warm contacts from newsletter | Founder | 2h | 20+ accepted invites by end of week |
| Extract exact language from 5 pilot alumni: what problem did they come in with, what specific outcome did they get, what would they say to a colleague considering it | Ops | 3h | 5 written quotes, in the alumni's own words, with permission to publish |
| Set up UTM tracking for all campaign links (newsletter, LinkedIn, GitHub, podcast show notes) in Plausible or PostHog | Ops | 2h | All channels tagged; dashboard live |
| Add waitlist enrollment section to GitHub README; link to waitlist landing page | Ops | 2h | README updated; landing page live with conversion tracking |
| Research 15 engineering podcast/newsletter targets: shows that cover AI engineering, senior practitioner audience, European or global reach; list contact channel and recent episode hook | Ops | 3h | Ranked list of 15 targets with contact method and pitch hook angle by Friday |

---

### Week 1

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Publish flagship LinkedIn post (Self/Us/Now community-first version): "Why 9 of 15 pilot students came from inside the group." Watch saves and comment depth for 48h. If saves >50 and substantive comments >10, boost with €150 to senior engineering audience in Western Europe. | Founder | 1.5h write + 0.5h monitor | Saves >50, comments >10 within 48h; if gate passes, boost within 72h |
| Send newsletter edition #1: "We published the curriculum. Here's why." — links to GitHub, introduces community, includes waitlist CTA | Founder | 2h | >35% open rate (baseline is 38%); >2% CTR to waitlist |
| Post daily in Slack/WhatsApp community for 7 consecutive days — practitioner observations, questions, reactions to what engineers are sharing | Founder | 0.5h/day = 3.5h | Thread replies from at least 3 members per day by day 5 |
| Pitch top 3 podcast targets from the researched list; personalised to each host's recent episode (Tuesday morning, email) | Ops | 2h | 1 confirmed booking or positive reply within 10 days |
| Publish Skeptic's First Week #1 on LinkedIn — specific tool evaluated, specific verdict, honest about failure | Founder | 1h | Saves >30 (half of flagship post as the baseline for a recurring format) |

---

### Week 2

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open curriculum release: publish GitHub repo section / separate public doc with the full AI engineering curriculum. Post on LinkedIn and newsletter. | Founder + Ops | 2h (founder write) + 1h (ops) | 200+ GitHub stars within 7 days; waitlist signups from README >20 |
| Send newsletter edition #2: "The curriculum is free. What you are paying for is the room." — introduces the 40-seat cohort and the waitlist | Founder | 1.5h | Waitlist CTR >3% from newsletter |
| Post daily in community (days 8–14) — this is the non-negotiable 14-day founder daily post sprint | Founder | 0.5h/day = 3.5h | Community member count >40; at least 5 members posting unprompted by day 14 |
| Pitch next 4 podcast targets from list; follow up on week 1 pitches | Ops | 2h | 2 total confirmed bookings |
| Publish Skeptic's First Week #2 on LinkedIn | Founder | 1h | Consistent with week 1 benchmark |
| Add formal referral mechanic: pilot alumni each get a personal referral link; if a referred engineer enrolls, the alumni receives a one-year community membership extension or guest Q&A slot | Ops | 1.5h | Referral links distributed to all 15 pilot alumni |

**Week 2 total: ~17h — within ceiling.**

---

### Week 3

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transition community from founder-only posts to first recurring ritual: "Production Reality Thursday" — weekly thread where members share one AI integration that worked or failed in the past week | Founder (launches) + Ops (monitors) | 1h founder + 1h ops | >5 member contributions to first thread |
| Send newsletter edition #3: "Cohort 1 waitlist is open — here is what the 15 people who did this said about it." Publish 3 alumni transformation quotes (in their own language, from week 0 extraction). | Founder | 1.5h | Waitlist signups >30 cumulative |
| Publish LinkedIn post: "What we actually shipped in 8 weeks — three cohort stories" — carousel or thread using the 3 alumni quotes with specific production outcomes | Founder | 1.5h | Saves >60 (the strongest content should outperform prior posts as the social proof accumulates) |
| Pitch 5 remaining podcast targets; follow up on all prior pitches | Ops | 1.5h | 3+ total confirmed bookings |
| Publish Skeptic's First Week #3 on LinkedIn | Founder | 1h | Consistent benchmark |

---

### Week 4

| Action | Owner | Effort | Success signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cohort 1 enrollment opens to community members first (48-hour head start before newsletter and public announcement) | Founder (announcement) + Ops (enrollment admin) | 1h founder + 2h ops | >10 applications from community members in first 48h |
| Send newsletter edition #4: "Cohort 1 enrollment is open — 40 seats." Hard CTA. Deadline 2 weeks out. | Founder | 1h | >5% CTR to enrollment page from newsletter (baseline is 38% open × 2% CTR — this should lift at enrollment moment) |
| Publish LinkedIn post: "40 seats. Who is this for?" — specific audience description (senior engineers, European companies, production AI integration, not beginners) — explicit exclusion of people it is not for | Founder | 1.5h | Saves >80; organic applications referencing this post |
| Assess 48h traction on enrollment LinkedIn post. If saves >80 and click-through to enrollment >40, boost with €300 targeting senior software engineers in UK, Germany, Netherlands, France, Nordics (LinkedIn job title targeting). | Ops | 0.5h decision + 1h setup | Boost only if gate passes; target: 20 incremental applications from boosted spend |
| Hand off community "Production Reality Thursday" ritual to 2 engaged members as co-hosts | Founder | 0.5h | Confirmed co-hosts; first self-sustaining thread |
| Skeptic's First Week #4 on LinkedIn | Founder | 1h | Cumulative: 4-week baseline established for the recurring series |

**Week 4 total: ~13h — under ceiling. Slack absorbed by enrollment admin variability.**

---

**Community build block summary (non-negotiable cadence):**
- **Week 0:** Launch, 20 invites, rules written. Dead room before launch = worse than no room.
- **Weeks 1–2:** Founder posts daily. Every day. No exceptions. This is the escape-velocity sprint.
- **Weeks 3–4:** Hand off two recurring rituals to 2–3 engaged members. If no members step up by week 4, the community is a broadcast channel in disguise — surface this as a validation problem, not a moderation problem.
- **Month 2+:** 3–4h/week sustained for moderation, weekly post, member welcomes.

---

## 9. Ad Copy + Boost Rules

**Paid applies at mild-asymmetry level against cohort competitors (not platforms). Three boost windows: cohort 1 launch (week 4), cohort 2 launch (~month 4.5), cohort 3 launch (~month 7).**

### Creative direction

Follow the authenticity playbook — founder voice, first person, specific rather than polished. No stock imagery. No countdown timers. No "only X spots left" unless it is exactly true and there are fewer than 10 remaining (at which point it is a fact, not manufactured urgency).

**Sample LinkedIn post copy (enrollment opening — not templated, written in founder voice):**

> Cohort 1 opens today.
>
> 40 seats. Senior software engineers. 8 weeks. Practical AI integration — not tutorials, not vendor demos, not "build a chatbot in 30 minutes."
>
> The pilot ran with 15 engineers. 9 of the next students came from someone inside that group. That's the signal I care about.
>
> The curriculum is free on GitHub. The cohort costs €1,200. The difference is 39 other engineers who are doing the same work, honest about what's failing, and still in Slack when week 8 is over.
>
> If this is not for you, it's not for you. If it is, the link is in the first comment.

**Why it works:** no passive-voice "we are excited to," no CTA urgency theatre, no credential inflation. The exclusion ("if this is not for you, it's not for you") is a trust signal to a sceptical audience — it reads as confidence, not desperation. The referral stat is specific and verifiable.

### 24–48h Organic Traction Gate

**Non-negotiable:** Do not boost any post until it has accumulated organic saves >50 and substantive comments (replies that engage with the content, not "great post!") >10 within 48 hours. Likes do not count. A post that reaches 200 likes and 8 saves in 48h has no organic traction worth amplifying. A post with 60 saves and 12 substantive comments is ready to boost.

### Audience definition for boosted posts

- **First ring:** Retargeting — LinkedIn website visitors and people who engaged with prior posts (custom audience).
- **Second ring:** Lookalikes built from the newsletter subscriber list (upload to LinkedIn matched audiences).
- **Third ring only if rings 1–2 are exhausted:** Cold targeting — LinkedIn job title "Software Engineer / Software Developer / Backend Engineer / Staff Engineer / Principal Engineer" + Geography: UK, Germany, Netherlands, France, Nordic countries + Company size: 50–5,000.
- **Excluded from targeting:** "AI enthusiast" interest targeting (the wrong audience; attracts hype-chasers, not the hype-wary senior engineers the course serves).

### Frequency cap and refresh

- Cap impressions at 5 per person per 2-week campaign window. Beyond 5, conversion rates flatten and go negative; you are paying to annoy engineers who have already decided.
- Refresh creative at every cohort window (3 new posts, not the same post repeated). Alumni voice posts for cohort 2/3 launches; the testimonials are the creative, not a graphic.

### Do not bid on brand keywords without a lift test

The course is not yet established enough to have brand-search volume. If brand-search volume develops by cohort 2, run Template 4 (brand-keyword holdout) before bidding on brand terms — the eBay/Blake, Nosko & Tadelis evidence is clear that brand-keyword bidding cannbalises free organic traffic. Do not pre-empt the test.

---

## 10. Lift-Test / Measurement Plan

**Template selection:** Template 5 — Organic-source attribution (zero-budget / directional), supplemented by Template 4 (micro-lift) for the cohort-launch boosts.

**No geo-holdout is appropriate here:** the user is operating across multiple European countries without meaningful regional segmentation by country; a two-country geo-holdout would introduce confounders (different engineering community density, different podcast exposure) that exceed the signal.

### Template 5 — Organic-Source Attribution (running continuously)

**Hypothesis:** LinkedIn founder content drives a higher per-visitor conversion rate to waitlist/enrollment than email newsletter, and earned-media podcast appearances drive a higher-quality long-tail of engaged subscribers than either.

**Design:**
- UTM-tag every link: `utm_source=linkedin_post / newsletter / github_readme / podcast_[slug] / referral_[alumni-name]`, `utm_medium=organic`, `utm_campaign=cohort1 / cohort2 / cohort3`
- Track in Plausible or PostHog: conversions = waitlist submission or enrollment application submitted
- Run for 30-day windows aligned to each pre-cohort marketing period

**Decision rule after 30 days:**
- Top channel by conversion rate (conversions ÷ unique visitors): double weekly effort in next window
- Bottom 20% of channels by conversion rate: drop unless serving a secondary function (GitHub README serves SEO/credibility regardless of direct conversion)
- If overall conversion rate across all channels <0.5%: pause, re-examine the enrollment page and the offer framing before reinvesting in distribution

**Caveat stated out loud:** This is directional, not causal. A referral-tagged conversion from an alumni link may reflect a person who heard about the course three times before clicking — the tag shows the last click, not the full journey. Treat as allocation guidance, not proof of channel effectiveness.

### Template 4 — Micro-lift for cohort-launch boosts

**Hypothesis:** Boosted LinkedIn posts during the 2-week enrollment window drive incremental applications beyond the organic baseline.

**Design:**
- During enrollment week 1 (the boost window): add one question to the application form — "How did you first hear about this course?" with options: LinkedIn post / Newsletter / Podcast / GitHub / A colleague / Other.
- Track the percentage of applicants who credit the boosted post vs. organic sources.
- If <5% of applicants credit a paid ad source: the boost is not the conversion driver — stop or redirect. If >15% credit paid and the application quality is equivalent to organic applicants (same completion rate, same cohort-fit score): continue at the same scale for cohort 2.

**Decision threshold:**
- Continue boosting at €300–500/cohort launch if paid-credited applications ≥15% of total AND cost per incremental application is <€50 (€500 ÷ 10 incremental applications).
- Stop if paid-credited applications <5% of total (cannibalising organic) or cost per incremental application >€100.

---

## 11. Anti-Vanity Metric Dashboard

### Track these

| Metric | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Waitlist-to-application conversion rate (per cohort) | The offer-to-intent signal; if it drops cohort-over-cohort, something changed in messaging or audience fit |
| Application source (How did you hear about us) | Directional quality signal for channel allocation |
| Referral % of each cohort | Rider 1 (cohort-education) says the room is the product; if referrals are not growing cohort-over-cohort, the room is not working |
| Newsletter CTR to enrollment page (not open rate alone) | Open rate is a hygiene metric; CTR to enrollment is intent |
| LinkedIn post saves (not likes) | Saves signal "I want to come back to this" — the highest-quality organic engagement signal on LinkedIn |
| Podcast appearance → newsletter subscriber conversion rate | The earned-media channel's real yield; measure by UTM-tagged episode show notes links |
| Community member activation (posts within 30 days of joining) | A community where only the founder posts is a broadcast channel; track the ratio |
| Cohort NPS that translates to referrals | Ask separately: "Would you recommend this?" (NPS) and "Have you recommended this to a specific person?" (referral behaviour). They diverge and the second number is the one that matters |
| Alumni → advocate conversion (actively sharing cohort content or referring) | The referral flywheel metric; target: >50% of alumni post one public thing about their cohort experience |

### Ignore these

| Metric | Why it misleads |
|---|---|
| LinkedIn follower count | Follower growth is a lagging vanity signal; saves and conversion rate are the leading signals |
| Total impressions on any post | Reach above the first 5–6 impressions does not persuade; it accumulates without impact |
| Newsletter open rate alone | 38% is already above benchmark; the question is what they do after opening, not whether they open |
| Platform-reported ROAS on boosted posts | Attributed, not incremental. The micro-lift question on the application form is the honest number |
| GitHub star count as a marketing metric | Stars signal developer interest in the OSS tool, not course enrollment intent; useful as credibility evidence, not as a campaign KPI |
| Social media follower growth during the campaign | Confounded with everything; not a leading indicator of enrollment |
| CPM on boosted posts | You are not buying attention at scale; you are amplifying proven organic winners. If the organic post worked, the CPM is irrelevant. If it did not, no CPM makes it work |
| Webinar attendee count | The campaign is not using a webinar funnel; if this appears on a report, it means someone has started running a competitor's playbook rather than this one |

---

**Sector failure-mode warning (Rider 1):** The most common way this campaign underperforms is if the founder starts marketing the curriculum instead of the room. Tutorials are free. Peer accountability is not. Every metric that rises (GitHub stars, YouTube views of a course preview) while enrollment conversion stays flat is a signal the campaign has drifted toward information marketing. Check the positioning line — "We publish everything we know. We charge for the room." — against every piece of content before publishing.

---

*Confirm or adjust any assumption in this plan and the affected stages will be re-run. The highest-leverage adjustment: if the founder cannot commit to 30 consecutive days of community posting in weeks 1–2, drop Shape A and route to Shape B (earned-media-first), which does not require that sustained daily founder presence. That is a material change and should be decided before week 0.*
